examples.title: Ford-Fulkerson Pseudocode
examples.description: Basic Ford-Fulkerson algorithm using DFS to find augmenting paths
examples.code:
ALGORITHM FordFulkerson(G, s, t)
BEGIN
  // Initialize flow to 0 on all edges
  FOR each edge (u,v) in G.E DO
    f[u,v] ← 0
  END FOR

  // While there exists an augmenting path
  WHILE (PathExists(Gf, s, t)) DO
    // Find the path using DFS/BFS
    path ← FindPath(Gf, s, t)

    // Find bottleneck (minimum residual capacity)
    bottleneck ← ∞
    FOR each edge (u,v) in path DO
      bottleneck ← min(bottleneck, Cf[u,v])
    END FOR

    // Augment flow along the path
    FOR each edge (u,v) in path DO
      IF (u,v) is forward edge THEN
        f[u,v] ← f[u,v] + bottleneck
      ELSE  // backward edge
        f[v,u] ← f[v,u] - bottleneck
      END IF
    END FOR

    // Update residual graph Gf
    UpdateResidualGraph(G, f)
  END WHILE

  RETURN f
END
examples.explanation: The algorithm repeatedly finds an augmenting path in the residual graph and increases flow by the bottleneck value until no augmenting path exists. Each iteration updates both forward and backward residual capacities.

examples.title: Edmonds-Karp Algorithm (BFS-based)
examples.description: Edmonds-Karp implementation using BFS to find shortest augmenting paths
examples.code:
ALGORITHM EdmondsKarp(G, s, t)
BEGIN
  // Initialize flow to 0
  FOR each edge (u,v) in G.E DO
    f[u,v] ← 0
  END FOR

  // While augmenting path exists
  WHILE (TRUE) DO
    // Use BFS to find shortest path (in terms of edges)
    parent ← BFS(G, s, t)

    // If no path found, we're done
    IF (parent[t] = NIL) THEN
      BREAK
    END IF

    // Calculate bottleneck
    bottleneck ← ∞
    v ← t
    WHILE (v ≠ s) DO
      u ← parent[v]
      bottleneck ← min(bottleneck, Cf[u,v])
      v ← u
    END WHILE

    // Augment flow
    v ← t
    WHILE (v ≠ s) DO
      u ← parent[v]
      IF (u,v) is forward edge THEN
        f[u,v] ← f[u,v] + bottleneck
      ELSE
        f[v,u] ← f[v,u] - bottleneck
      END IF
      v ← u
    END WHILE
  END WHILE

  RETURN f
END

ALGORITHM BFS(G, s, t)
BEGIN
  Initialize queue with s
  visited[s] ← TRUE
  parent[v] ← NIL for all v

  WHILE queue not empty DO
    u ← dequeue()
    FOR each neighbor v of u IN Gf DO
      IF NOT visited[v] AND Cf[u,v] > 0 THEN
        visited[v] ← TRUE
        parent[v] ← u
        IF v = t THEN RETURN parent
        enqueue(v)
      END IF
    END FOR
  END WHILE

  RETURN parent
END
examples.explanation: Edmonds-Karp is Ford-Fulkerson using BFS to always find the shortest augmenting path (fewest edges). This guarantees polynomial time complexity O(VE²) as opposed to potentially exponential time of basic Ford-Fulkerson.

examples.title: Python Implementation of Ford-Fulkerson
examples.description: Complete Python implementation with BFS for finding augmenting paths
examples.code:
from collections import deque

def ford_fulkerson(graph, source, sink):
    """
    Ford-Fulkerson algorithm using BFS (Edmonds-Karp)
    graph: adjacency matrix or dict of dict
    source: source vertex
    sink: sink vertex
    """
    # Create residual graph
    residual = {u: {v: graph[u].get(v, 0) for v in graph} for u in graph}
    n = len(graph)
    max_flow = 0

    while True:
        # BFS to find augmenting path
        parent = {u: None for u in graph}
        parent[source] = source
        queue = deque([source])

        while queue and parent[sink] is None:
            u = queue.popleft()
            for v in graph[u]:
                if parent[v] is None and residual[u][v] > 0:
                    parent[v] = u
                    queue.append(v)
                    if v == sink:
                        break

        # If no augmenting path exists
        if parent[sink] is None:
            break

        # Find bottleneck value
        bottleneck = float('inf')
        v = sink
        while v != source:
            u = parent[v]
            bottleneck = min(bottleneck, residual[u][v])
            v = u

        # Update residual capacities
        v = sink
        while v != source:
            u = parent[v]
            residual[u][v] -= bottleneck
            residual[v][u] += bottleneck
            v = u

        max_flow += bottleneck

    return max_flow

# Example usage
graph = {
    's': {'a': 10, 'b': 5},
    'a': {'t': 8, 'b': 3},
    'b': {'t': 4},
    't': {}
}
print(f"Maximum flow: {ford_fulkerson(graph, 's', 't')}")
examples.explanation: This Python implementation uses BFS (Edmonds-Karp) to find augmenting paths. It maintains a residual graph and updates capacities in both forward and backward directions. The algorithm returns the maximum flow value.
